does: This paper constructs minimal mathematical models of neural networks that exhibit polysemanticity—the phenomenon where individual neurons encode multiple unrelated concepts—without requiring realistic scale or complexity. The authors demonstrate that networks naturally compress sparse features into superposition, where many features share the same neurons through careful geometric alignment. They identify a sharp phase transition marking the boundary between regimes where superposition is beneficial versus harmful, establish unexpected mathematical connections to uniform polytopes, and show that superposed representations become more vulnerable to adversarial perturbations. The work provides complete theoretical understanding of when and why this compression strategy emerges as an optimal solution.
connects: This work directly addresses long-standing challenges in neural network interpretability, building on decades of frustration with polysemanticity as an obstacle to mechanistic understanding. It extends classical sparse coding literature by studying how networks resolve the tension between representing many sparse features with limited neurons. The geometric insights connect to work on neural network loss landscapes and phase transitions in learning, while the adversarial vulnerability link bridges interpretability research with robustness literature. The paper responds to earlier scattered observations of feature mixing in real networks by providing rigorous toy model analysis, following the successful tradition of using simplified settings (like grokking models) to understand large-scale phenomena.
enables: This framework opens pathways for designing more interpretable architectures by leveraging understanding of superposition's underlying mechanics and cost-benefit tradeoffs. The geometric characterization suggests new methods for detecting and potentially disentangling superposed features in real networks. The phase transition structure provides theoretical handles for controlling feature compression in practice. The connection to adversarial robustness hints that improving mechanistic interpretability might require architectural changes that reduce superposition, or conversely that superposition represents a fundamental limitation. Future work can build on these toy models to study which architectural constraints prevent problematic superposition while preserving network expressiveness and efficiency.
